- The video discusses Tuberculosis (TB), also known as consumption.

- At 16:11, a quote appears: "waywardness, peevishness, irascibility, misanthropy, [and] murky passions... are referable to their constitutional peculiarities and condition: In simple words that their mental eccentricities result from the derangement of bodily health." This suggests a link between the disease and mental/creative states.
- The video mentions famous artists and writers who had TB, like Lord Byron, John Keats, Edgar Allan Poe, etc. (around 13:00-18:00).
- It discusses "consumptive chic" (18:41), where the symptoms of TB (pale skin, thinness) were considered fashionable.
- Option A says "Roughly 30% of all northern Europeans were dying of TB". The video mentions that 1 in 4 people in Europe died of TB at some point, or similar statistics, but "most of them were artists and writers" is definitely false. It affected everyone, though artists/writers were romanticized.
- Option B says "The consumption of TB was considered a disease of the soul, and it was believed that the disease made people more creative." The video explicitly links TB to the soul (breath/spirit) and discusses how it was thought to heighten sensitivity and creativity (the "consumptive chic" and the idea that it made people look interesting or profound). The quote at 16:11 supports the idea that mental eccentricities (often associated with creativity) were linked to the disease.
- Option C says treatment was primarily based on antibiotics in the 19th century. Antibiotics were discovered in the 1940s (Streptomycin), so this is chronologically impossible for the 19th century. The video discusses sanatoriums and fresh air as 19th-century treatments.
- Option D says TB only affected the poor. While it affected the poor heavily due to living conditions, the video shows it affected rich people too (royalty, famous writers/artists). So "only" makes this false.

- "believed that the disease made people more creative": The video discusses how TB was associated with heightened sensitivity, passion, and creativity in artists and writers (Byron, Keats, etc.). The quote at 16:11 explicitly links "mental eccentricities" to the "derangement of bodily health" caused by TB.
